findings.message: Logging the user's email address (PII) at INFO level is a GDPR violation: personal data is written into application logs with no retention/deletion path, no pseudonymisation, and no lawful-basis audit trail. Logs typically have long retention and are exported to multiple sinks, putting this PII outside the reach of erasure (Art. 17) and minimisation (Art. 5(1)(c)) obligations.
findings.suggestion: Do not log raw email. Either omit the identifier, hash/pseudonymise it (e.g. HMAC-SHA256 with a rotated key), or log only an opaque internal user id. If the call genuinely needs an audit record, route it through a dedicated audit log with documented retention, access controls, and an erasure hook — not the general application logger.

findings.message: Accepting an `email` query parameter as the personalisation key creates a personal-data processing surface with no documented lawful basis, no retention policy, and no right-to-erasure path. Passing email in the URL also causes it to be captured by access logs, proxies, and browser history.
findings.suggestion: Identify the caller via an authenticated session/user id rather than an email query parameter; if email truly must be accepted, send it in the request body over POST, scrub it from access logs, and tie it to a documented retention + erasure workflow.

findings.message: `search_items` is a sensitive operation (user-identified personalisation lookup) but has no audit log entry — only an INFO application log that conflates audit with operational logging. There is no tamper-resistant record of who searched what, which is required for accountability under GDPR Art. 5(2) and common audit regimes.
findings.suggestion: Emit a structured audit event via a dedicated audit sink (separate from application logs) recording actor (pseudonymised id), action, resource, timestamp, and correlation id; define retention for the audit log distinct from app logs.
